title: Failure as not-fitting-in — the bass-thump-behind-you frequency theory
html:
Failure as not-fitting-in — the bass-thump-behind-you frequency theory

Source: 2022-01-07 Apple Note, ~6am. Title alone is a thesis: "Failure is terrifying and humiliating. It means not fitting in." Yahir at 19, mid-OSU winter term, working out his outsider thesis.

TL;DR

  • Failure reframed as social: not failure-as-loss but failure-as-exile — "It means not fitting in." The penalty isn't the missed outcome, it's the loss of belonging. This is a fear of not doing-adjacent frame from a different angle.
  • The Tetris-photo aside: "Tetris photo does have credibility" — opaque to the reader, likely a private reference (a screenshot of a Tetris score, or a meme). Yahir grants it credibility. Left as-is.
  • Outsider observation: "No one else I've met has not fit in." He hasn't yet found another outsider in his immediate world.
  • The frequency hypothesis: maybe other outsiders do exist, and they've found "people to whom they are on the same frequency with." He guesses where they cluster: "Is it Silicon Valley, is it in jail. Different kinds of people." The pairing is the tell — Yahir doesn't romanticize Silicon Valley alone; he names jail in the same breath. Outsiders cluster wherever the frequency matches, regardless of social tier.
  • The bass-thump image: "Always will be a place where you fit in, always never likely feels right. Like a massive bass behind you thumping." The found-frequency is felt as low-end resonance behind the body, not seen. A somatic image of belonging.

Pattern observed

This is the founding outsider-thesis note in the vault — the earliest articulation of the frame Yahir keeps returning to. The Austin thesis is partly about finding the bass-thump (the frequency-tribe). The China chapter is about deliberately stepping into a place where the frequency cannot match by language, then noticing what you become. The am I a concept doubt is partly about whether he's manufactured a frequency to attract people rather than waiting to feel one.

The Silicon-Valley-or-jail bracket is also the seed of his much later spy/leader/creator archetype thinking — outsiders go to either pole; the middle is where fit-in pressure crushes them.

Key quotes

  • "Failure is terrifying and humiliating. It means not fitting in."
  • "Is it Silicon Valley, is it in jail. Different kinds of people."
  • "Like a massive bass behind you thumping."
